mappings.rationaleAI: The Boxer Rebellion (1899-1901), anti-Christian and anti-foreign sentiment, the siege of the Legation Quarter, international military response, and Empress Dowager Cixi's role directly correspond to A.2.c (Boxer Rebellion as expression of anti-foreign feelings and Western seizure of Peking).

Empress Dowager Cixi's management of the Tongzhi and Guangxu reigns, the Self-Strengthening Movement, the Hundred Days' Reform and its suppression, and her role in the Boxer Rebellion correspond to A.2.a (self-strengthening movement) and A.2.c-d (conservative reaction and reform movements).

The Opium Wars (1839-1842, 1856-1860), the Treaty of Nanking, and the unequal treaty system directly correspond to A.1.a on the Western challenge (1839-60) and the problems created by the opium trade.

The Taiping Rebellion (1850-1864), Hong Xiuquan's Christianised ideology, the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom's radical programme, and the Qing suppression directly correspond to A.1.b on popular uprisings of the Taiping and the effects of the rebellions.

The Korean Empire (1897-1910) and the Sino-Japanese and Russo-Japanese Wars' impact on Korean sovereignty correspond to A.2.b (Korea and the Sino-Japanese War, 1894-95) and the broader context of foreign encroachments.
Japan's Sakoku period and its ending by Commodore Perry provide the essential precursor to B.1.a on the fall of the Tokugawa and the Meiji Restoration.

The Russo-Japanese War (1904-1905), Japan's victory as an Asian power defeating a European one, and the Treaty of Portsmouth correspond to Japan's emergence as a world power under B.1 and its growing military and political significance.

While the Second Sino-Japanese War (1937-1945) is chronologically later, it connects to the broader Sino-Japanese tensions beginning with the 1894-95 war (A.2.b). However, this is largely outside the section's timeframe.
The samurai class's abolition during the Meiji Restoration directly corresponds to B.1.a on the fall of the Tokugawa, samurai opposition, and government countermeasures during the early Meiji period.

The May Fourth Movement (1919) is chronologically just beyond this section but its roots in anti-imperialist sentiment and the New Culture Movement challenging Confucianism directly follow from A.2.d on reformist and revolutionary movements at the end of the dynasty.
The Shimabara Rebellion's consequences (sakoku policy) provide essential historical context for understanding B.1.a on the fall of the Tokugawa and the end of Japanese isolation.
